---
title: "Lecture 1"
format: html
---

## Motivating this Course

Let's consider a world where statistics doesn't exist. What would it look like? People could make random claims without numerical evidence. Your feed would look like a random mess of videos constantly being posted. One could argue that we couldn't count the number of people enrolled in this course.

Ok, well that seemed pretty obvious. Of course, the reason for taking this course is likely some form of personal interest, as it's a random half credit house course. Perhaps you're a CS major who wants to learn some statistical algorithms to recreate Google's PageRank. Maybe you're a premed and hope to do a survival analysis to support your research interests. Finally, maybe you're a sociologist and want to discover a population parameter about the use of AI in the classroom.

Let's reflect on what today's world looks like. A reasonable statement would be: "Statistics exists. Some people know how to use statistics, some other people sort of know what statistics is, and a vast majority of people have heard of it." The goal of this course is to move from the third group to the second group, in a way that could set you up for self learning to make it to the first group. We also hope that you'll be able to critically evaluate the quality of a statistical analysis on a paper you've never seen before. There is much to learn! 

## What is statistics? What is a statistic?

Statistics (the field) is a mathematical science pertaining to the collection, analysis, interpretation, and presentation of data.

A statistic is any quantity computed from values in a sample. That is incredibly broad. During this course, we will primarily focus on two types of statistics (which are not mutually exclusive of one another):
1. Descriptive statistics, which summarize a sample of data
2. Test statistics, which is some number derived from the sample that would be selected or defined in a way that differentiates between the null and alternative hypothesis.
